title: Deuteronomy: Leadership and War
objective: Deepen biblical-theological understanding of deuteronomy: leadership and war and its place in redemptive history.

required_passages.text: Selected key passages for this unit segment, read in full during lesson delivery.

segments.audio_script: Today we study deuteronomy: leadership and war and trace God's covenant faithfulness.
Listen to the assigned passage and note covenant themes, divine actions, and human response.
We place this text in its historical setting, canonical location, and literary flow.
We analyze theological meaning, covenant development, and connections to the wider biblical story.
Key themes include God's holiness, covenant faithfulness, judgment and mercy, and redemptive purpose.

Review question: What three things did the law of the king in Deuteronomy 17 forbid Israel's future king from doing? Answer: acquiring many horses, acquiring many wives, and accumulating excessive silver and gold.
segments.correct_answer: not multiply horses, not multiply wives, and not accumulate excessive silver and gold
acceptable_variants: no many horses — no many wives — no excessive wealth
the king must not multiply horses wives or money
three limits on the king: horses, wives, and wealth

segments.audio_script: Carry this lesson into prayer, worship, and careful Scripture reading as we continue the course.

vocabulary.definition: Deuteronomy 17:14-20's regulations for Israel's future king; he must not multiply horses, wives, or wealth, and must copy and read the law daily — a standard Solomon violates in all three areas
The king's command in Deuteronomy 17:18 to write out a copy of the law for himself and read it daily so he learns to fear God and not elevate himself above his brothers
